dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 72-year-old male with chest pain on Summit Street.
Hi sir, I'm Carla and this is Jordan, our EMT. What made you call 911?
Sharp left-sided chest pain, like a knife, that comes and goes.
Let me check your alertness. Can you tell me what day it is?
The 20th. I woke up with this pain and couldn't get relief from anything.
And the city we're in?
[CITY], [STATE]. That's where I've been living for ten years.
How about where you were born? Just so I can confirm your orientation.
Minnesota. I was actually born there, yes.
Pt is awake and speaking coherently-oriented to person, place, time, and situation (AAOX4), GCS 15 (E4, V5, M6).
Your airway is patent and you're breathing without difficulty.
Your breathing isn't showing signs of distress. You're taking controlled breaths.
Respirations are steady: 16 at first and now down to 13. Let me just note that.
Pulse slows from 75 at first to 60 now. Strength and regularity are good.
Blood pressure was up at 152/84 and now it's 120/69-still within normal range.
Oxygen saturation at 99% with room air, and now it's at 96%.
Blood sugar was 103 mg/dL initially, then measured at 0 mg/dL later.
EKG indicates sinus rhythm at 75 bpm before and now shows 60 bpm.
Your skin is warm, dry, and normal in color. Do you have any numbness or color changes elsewhere?

No, I haven't felt any unusual temperature changes or visual changes to my skin. 21. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; EMT: Tell me a bit more about your chest pain. 22. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: It started as I was lying in bed around 2 a.m. and it's totally sharp and stabbing.

Did you experience something like this before? 24. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: A few months ago. Took some omeprazole then and that worked, but not this time.
What meds are you on daily?
I take metoprolol and omeprazole.

Have you taken your metoprolol today? 28. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; Patient: Yes. It's always been a scheduled dose I take. 29. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; EMT: What's your medical history? 30. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Patient: Pulmonary hypertension and GERD.
Do you have any known drug allergies?
I don't have any known allergies.

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Since the pain started, what have you done to manage it? 34. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; Patient: I took some omeprazole but didn't feel any better. That's when I decided to call 911.

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Please walk us through what led to the pain. 36. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; Patient: It just hit me hard while I was in bed, and it's been lingering. I tried sitting up, but it didn't improve.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how severe is your chest pain right now?
It's a 10-unrelenting and sharp.
How would you describe the pain specifically?
It feels like a stabbing pain that fluctuates.
Have you noticed this pain radiating anywhere else-like your neck, jaw, or arms?
No, it's entirely contained in my left chest.
How long has this been happening? 44. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: time; Patient: Around 30 minutes now, so it's been a while.
What makes the pain worse or better?
It comes and goes, but there's no point where it eases up at all.
Skin is normal in color and texture. No signs of rash or visible trauma.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No obstruction or drainage detected from your nose or ears.
Your pupils are 3 mm, equal, and reactive; no signs of neck swelling or masses.
I see you've taken some aspirin. Can we administer an extra dose to reach the standard 324 mg?
Yes, I'll agree to that.
Administering 243 mg oral aspirin. Double-checked by both providers for safety.
Would you allow us to place you on a cardiac monitor?
Please go ahead.
Starting 4-lead ECG monitoring. This will monitor your heart's activity.
We're also going to place an IV in your right arm for possible future meds or fluids.
I'm up for that.
IV has been placed in your right antecubital vein, and we've secured it with a saline lock.
Performing 12-lead ECG first, standard part of chest pain protocols.
Following the Suspected Cardiac Chest Pain EMS protocol due to symptoms and vital changes.
Latest vitals: pulse 60, blood pressure 120/69, O2 saturation 96%.
EKG consistent with sinus rhythm. Glucose remains at 0.
IV is in good position. EKG shows no signs of acute change.
Where would you prefer to be taken for treatment?
I want to go to [COMPANY_NAME], please.
Hospital has been notified. They've accepted your patient status and will prepare for arrival.
Can you walk over to the stretcher with a little assistance from us?
Yes, I can manage it.
Grab my shoulder there to help guide your balance.
Taking you carefully over to the stretcher now.
Positioned and secured with a five-point restraint system.
All set. Just let us know if anything concerning changes with your symptoms.
Still a 10-no relief, honestly.
Got that. Please, if you feel faint or notice changes in your breathing, tell us immediately.
Interventions remain stable. EKG and vitals unchanged during transport so far.
Pulse is still 60, BP 120/69. Everything is staying within normal parameters.
Transport en route per protocol. You might feel some minor bumps, but we'll stay with it.
I'm aware and I'll watch for changes.
ER is aware of your status and any upcoming changes will be reported promptly.
Keep us in the loop if something seems off.
Slightly adjusting your stretcher height for greater comfort during the ride.
Yeah, that seems better.
Position feels better. If you experience new symptoms or worsening pain, please say so.
Let us know at once if you have any new issues.
Final vitals read 120/69, 60, 96%-everything is in check.
We're almost at the hospital.
Stretcher is now locked in for internal transition.
Patient transition completed. He remains stable with no changes in condition.
Handing over a 72-year-old male with persistent chest pain to ER personnel.
IV and EKG have been properly prepared for transport into the hospital.
There's been no change in EKG or vitals from start to finish.
All steps per protocol have been completed and documented. Transfer is complete.
Transport was smooth and per protocol. No changes in condition were noted.
Everything's complete for handover. All clear to move forward with hospital care.
